title_tesim: Charles Laughton, Sally Forrest, Richard Stapley, and William Cottrell in The Strange Door
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph:From: U-I, Universal City, Calif."THE STRANGE DOOR" --- A Pictorial Synopsis"The Strange Door" is an adaptation of the Robert Louis Stevenson story, "The Sire de Maletroit's Door." It is an 18th Century thriller, told on the screen with all stops out. For the sake of expertness and to keep its audiences pinned on seat-edge, its principal roles are played by CHARLES LAUGHTON and BORIS KARLOFF, old hands at this sort of thing. SALLY FORREST and RICHARD STAPLEY are starred in romantic roles. Screenplay is by Jerry Sackheim, direction by Joseph Pevney, production by Ted Richmond for Universal-International. The story in pictures: 8. Denis is recaptured by Alan, and, together with Blanche, is escorted by Alan and Corbeau to the dungeons for the final act of this drama of revenge. Enroute Alan shows them a giant water wheel which, when thrown into gear, moves the wall of the cell wherein Blanche's father is prisoner. Blanche meets her father for the first time and is placed in the cell with him and her bridegroom - to be crushed to death!
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

For the sake of expertness and to keep its audiences pinned on seat-edge, its principal roles are played by CHARLES LAUGHTON and BORIS KARLOFF, old hands at this sort of thing. SALLY FORREST and RICHARD STAPLEY are starred in romantic roles. Screenplay is by Jerry Sackheim, direction by Joseph Pevney, production by Ted Richmond for Universal-International. The story in pictures: 2. Owner of the chateau is one of the most influential nobles of France, Alan, the Sire de Maledroit (CHARLES LAUGHTON), a cruel, conniving man subject to fits of insanity, guilty of many crimes and now plotting the ultimate crime of his life. Denis finds himself treated like a guest - yet he is a prisoner.

title_tesim: William Bendix on the set of Blaze of Noon
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Rugged Indoor Flying---The life of a pilot in a movie is not always a simple one in spite of the much vaunted tricks of the trade. Here William Bendix, who plays a mail pilot given to unorthodox stunting in the Paramount aviation epic, "Blaze of Noon," is the victim as closeups of an upside down flight are photographed inside a stage at the studio. The indoor scenes matched exteriors showing a plane going through violent maneuvers. Bill stars with Anne Baxter, William Holden, Sonny Tufts, Sterling Hayden and Howard da Silva in the airmail story directed by John Farrow.(1) With a special pad around his hips, William Bendix is fitted into the rig which will hold him upside down in a stunting plane, the instrument panel of which can be seen above him. Two "wire" men, who arranged the harness, hold Bill before he's hoisted.(Please Credit "Blaze of Noon")
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: William Bendix (center) and two crew members on the set of "Blaze of Noon."

title_tesim: Horse and rider in a scene from Calamity Jane and Sam Bass
uniform_title_tesim: Calamity Jane and Sam Bass (Motion picture)
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: "Calamity Jane and Sam Bass" - A Pictorial Synopsis"Calamity Jane and Sam Bass" is a Technicolor western, a tale of the Old West when each man, and an occasional woman, carried the law in a holster on his hip. Its story is a heady mixture of guns and girls, written originally by George Sherman, who directed, and written as a screenplay by Maurice Geraghty and Melvin Levy. Starring Yvonne De Carlo as Calamity Jane and Howard Duff as Sam Bass, its other top players are Dorothy Hart, Willard Parker and Lloyd Bridges. Leonard Goldstein produced, and Irving Glassberg directed photography, the footage having been shot almost entirely in southern Utah. The story in pictures:4. When the race is run, the mare takes the lead, then falters and falls, making it possible for Calamity and Thunderbolt to catch up and win. Sam collects, and Calamity gets mighty interested in him, as a man and judge of horse flesh. The mare's owner, thinking she has pulled a tendon, sells her to Sam for $200. Sheriff Eagan learns what Sam did with the borrowed $50, decides he is a liar and a crook and fires him off the ranch. Sam leaves a note for Kathy saying he'll return.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: Horse and rider in a scene from Calamity Jane and Sam Bass.

McAfee got a try-out this season with the Cubs after having pitched at the University of Michigan. Moss was a Vanderbilt College chucker who has been with Louisville in the American Association and was purchased by Chicago this year. Moss was a member of Phi Beta Kappa, national honorary scholastic society, at Vanderbilt and is a graduate in law from the University of Chicago.

title_tesim: Dana Andrews in Sword In The Desert
uniform_title_tesim: Sword In The Desert, The  (Motion picture)
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph:From: Universal-InternationalUniversal City, Calif."SWORD IN THE DESERT" - A Pictorial SynopsisFirst movie laid against a background of modern Palistine, "Sword in the Desert" is one of the topical bannerline films of the year and will be subject to much discussion. Its rapid-fire story capsuled into a 48-hour period on Dec. 23-24, 1947, during the British mandate over Palestine, it is based on a series of factual incidents. Its conflict is not political argumentation between British and Jews, but a story of action - a chase involving British patrols, Jewish immigrants and underground fighters, an American freighter captain - and, of course, a girl. Starring Dana Andrews, Marta Toren and Stephen McNally, it was written and produced by Robert Buckner and directed by George Sherman. The story in pictures:11. That night, as underground fighters deploy about the British stockade, two of them, disguised as Arab magicians, enter the stockade and shoot out the protecting searchlights. This is the signal for a general attack from within and without, and all underground prisoners break out of their pens. Dillon, Kurta and the rest gain their freedom, but McCarthy and Sorrell are killed by British fire.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Claudette Colbert in Zaza
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: PIQANT– in the most piquant role she has yet attempted, Claudette Colbert wears the frills of the early 1900's, sings and dances the can-can in Paramount's "Zaza," in which she is now starring with Herbert Marshall opposite her. A fascinating music hall entertainer, she wins Marshall away from his wife in the picture, only to give him back. The picture is antique in setting but has a modern day spice and the picturesque charm and vivacity of the theater in its heyday.

title_tesim: Elizabeth Taylor in Elephant Walk
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Elizabeth Taylor, who co-stars with Dana Andrews and Peter Finch in Paramount's "Elephant Walk," models this magnificent Edith Head evening gown. Miss Head was influenced by the locale of the film, and the gown is East Indian in treatment. It is white chiffon with a one-sided draped bodice like an East Indian Sari that flows into the draped, looped skirt. The loop is also one-sided in the skirt which gives a continuity of the flowing line. With it Miss Taylor wears a fabulously jeweled matching gold and pearl earrings and upper-arm bracelet are also from East India. Note that the bodice ends in a long, flowing scarf that also resembles the Sari.
Elizabeth Taylor in Paramount Pictures.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
